enrichment.short_description: Commure is a healthcare technology company that builds an AI-powered operating system, CommureOS, for hospitals and health systems. It provides ambient clinical documentation, revenue cycle management, patient engagement, remote monitoring, mobile EHR access, and staff safety tools used across hundreds of care facilities in the United States.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom enterprise pricing, sales-led (quote-based, contracted with health systems)
enrichment.starting_price: Not publicly disclosed; enterprise contracts negotiated per health system
enrichment.company_size: Roughly 700 to 1,500 employees (estimates vary by source, larger after the Athelas and Augmedix combinations)
enrichment.seo_title: Commure Review: Healthcare AI Platform Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Commure is an AI-powered healthcare operating system for clinical documentation, revenue cycle, and patient workflows. See features, pricing, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Commure is a privately held healthcare technology company headquartered in Mountain View, California, founded in 2017. It builds CommureOS, a unified software platform that health systems use to run clinical, administrative, and financial workflows on a single operating layer rather than stitching together disconnected point solutions. In October 2023, Commure announced a combination with Athelas, a healthcare workflow automation company, in a deal backed by a 70 million dollar investment from General Catalyst that valued the combined entity at roughly 6 billion dollars; the merged company retained the Commure name and is led by Athelas co-founder Tanay Tandon as CEO. By 2026 Commure's valuation had grown to approximately 7 billion dollars, with backing from investors including General Catalyst, Sequoia Capital, NVIDIA, and Y Combinator. Commure has also expanded through further acquisitions, including a deal to acquire Augmedix, a NASDAQ-listed ambient AI documentation company, taking it private and further building out its AI capabilities in healthcare. As of 2026 the combined company reaches more than 160,000 clinicians and staff across more than 500 care facilities in the United States. Commure's product suite spans multiple categories built on top of CommureOS: Ambient AI for real-time clinical documentation during patient visits, Dictation for voice-to-text charting, Call Center Agents for automated scheduling and call routing, Orchestrator for AI-assisted referral intake, RCM (Revenue Cycle Management) for medical billing automation and claims workflows, Engage for patient experience and care coordination, Commure Pro as a broader clinical intelligence platform for hospital operations, Strongline for staff safety alerting and nurse badging, and Athelas Home for point-of-care blood diagnostics. The platform is aimed at hospital and health system IT leaders, revenue cycle teams, clinicians, and care coordinators who need to reduce administrative burden, cut documentation time, and improve throughput without replacing their core electronic health record (EHR) system. Commure integrates with existing EHR systems rather than replacing them, positioning itself as an infrastructure and automation layer that sits above legacy hospital software. Pricing is not publicly published; Commure sells through enterprise sales cycles with contracts negotiated individually per health system based on facility size, modules selected, and integration scope. Competitors in adjacent categories include ambient AI scribe vendors like Nuance DAX and Abridge, revenue cycle vendors like Waystar, and broader health IT platforms. Because Commure operates in a regulated industry, its products are built with HIPAA compliance and clinical data security as core requirements. Buyers evaluating Commure should expect a multi-stakeholder enterprise sales process involving IT, compliance, clinical leadership, and finance, typical of large-scale health system software procurement, and should request references from health systems of comparable size before committing to a full CommureOS deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Commure
paragraphs: Commure is a healthcare technology company that provides CommureOS, a software operating system designed to sit on top of a hospital's existing electronic health record and unify clinical, administrative, and financial workflows in one place. Rather than selling a single point product, Commure organizes its offerings around distinct workflow categories, including ambient clinical documentation, revenue cycle management, patient engagement, remote patient monitoring, mobile access to EHR data, and physical staff safety.

The company was founded in 2017 and is headquartered in Mountain View, California. Its growth accelerated significantly after a 2023 combination with Athelas, a healthcare workflow automation company, which brought the combined business to a reported valuation of about 6 billion dollars, and later grew further following the acquisition of ambient AI documentation company Augmedix.

long_form_blocks.heading: Commure Product Suite
paragraphs: Commure's flagship AI features include Ambient AI, which listens to and documents patient encounters in real time, and Dictation, a voice-to-cursor charting tool that reduces manual note-writing. On the operational side, Call Center Agents automate scheduling and inbound call routing, and Orchestrator applies AI to referral intake so care coordinators spend less time on manual triage.

For the financial side of hospital operations, Commure RCM automates medical billing and claims workflows to reduce denials and speed up reimbursement. Engage focuses on patient experience and care coordination, while Strongline provides staff safety alerting and nurse badging technology originally developed to protect frontline healthcare workers. Athelas Home extends the platform into point-of-care blood diagnostics for remote patient monitoring use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and How Health Systems Buy Commure
paragraphs: Commure does not publish self-service pricing. As an enterprise health IT vendor, it sells through a sales-led process where pricing depends on facility size, the number of modules deployed, integration complexity with the existing EHR, and contract length. Prospective customers typically need to go through discovery calls, security and compliance review, and a pilot phase before a system-wide rollout.

Because Commure handles protected health information, any deployment involves HIPAA-compliant data handling and typically a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) between the health system and Commure as part of the contracting process.

features.title: CommureOS unified platform
features.description: A single operating layer that connects clinical, administrative, and financial workflows across a health system rather than requiring separate disconnected tools.

features.title: Ambient AI clinical documentation
features.description: Captures patient-provider conversations and generates structured clinical notes automatically to reduce time spent on manual charting.

features.title: Voice dictation
features.description: Voice-to-cursor dictation lets clinicians enter notes and orders hands-free directly into supported systems.

features.title: AI call center agents
features.description: Automates appointment scheduling and inbound call routing for front-desk and call center operations.

features.title: Revenue cycle management (RCM)
features.description: Automates medical billing, coding assistance, and claims workflows to reduce denials and accelerate reimbursement.

features.title: Referral intake automation (Orchestrator)
features.description: Applies AI to incoming referrals to reduce manual triage work for care coordination teams.

features.title: Patient engagement (Engage)
features.description: Tools for patient communication, scheduling reminders, and care coordination to improve the patient experience.

features.title: Staff safety and asset tracking (Strongline)
features.description: Wearable badge technology for nurse and staff safety alerting, plus asset tracking within facilities.

features.title: Remote diagnostics (Athelas Home)
features.description: Point-of-care blood diagnostic devices that support remote patient monitoring programs.

pros: Broad, integrated platform reduces the need to buy and stitch together many separate point solutions
Backed by major investors including General Catalyst, Sequoia, and NVIDIA, suggesting funding stability for a private company
Strong recent growth through acquisitions of Athelas and Augmedix has broadened AI documentation capabilities
Designed to integrate with existing EHR systems rather than requiring a costly EHR replacement
Covers both clinical (documentation, diagnostics) and operational (billing, safety) use cases under one vendor relationship
Serves a large existing base of clinicians and care facilities, indicating proven enterprise deployment experience
cons: Pricing is not public, which makes it hard for smaller practices to gauge affordability before entering a sales process
As a fast-growing, acquisition-heavy company, product lines and branding have changed multiple times, which can create integration and support continuity questions
Enterprise sales cycles for health systems tend to be long, involving procurement, security review, and IT integration timelines
Being a private company with a high valuation, long-term independence versus further M and A activity is uncertain
Broad product suite may mean some individual modules are less mature than dedicated single-purpose competitors in that category
Detailed public information on uptime, support SLAs, and implementation timelines is limited outside of sales conversations

faqs.question: What does Commure do
faqs.answer: Commure builds CommureOS, a healthcare software platform that automates clinical documentation, revenue cycle management, patient engagement, remote monitoring, and staff safety workflows for hospitals and health systems.

faqs.question: Is Commure the same company as Athelas
faqs.answer: Athelas and Commure combined in 2023 into a single company that retained the Commure name and brand, led by former Athelas CEO Tanay Tandon.

faqs.question: How much does Commure cost
faqs.answer: Commure does not publish pricing publicly. It sells through custom enterprise contracts negotiated with each health system based on facility size and modules deployed.

faqs.question: Does Commure replace a hospital's EHR system
faqs.answer: No. Commure is designed to integrate with and sit on top of a health system's existing electronic health record rather than replace it.

faqs.question: Who owns Commure
faqs.answer: Commure is a privately held company backed by investors including General Catalyst, Sequoia Capital, NVIDIA, and Y Combinator, with a reported valuation of roughly 7 billion dollars as of 2026.

faqs.question: What is Strongline
faqs.answer: Strongline is Commure's staff safety product, providing wearable alerting badges and asset tracking to protect frontline healthcare workers.

faqs.question: Has Commure acquired other companies
faqs.answer: Yes. Commure combined with Athelas in 2023 and signed a deal to acquire Augmedix, a publicly traded ambient AI documentation company, expanding its AI healthcare capabilities.

faqs.question: Is Commure HIPAA compliant
faqs.answer: As a healthcare technology vendor handling protected health information, Commure operates under HIPAA-compliant data handling practices and typically enters into Business Associate Agreements with health system customers.

use_cases.title: Reducing clinician documentation burden
use_cases.description: Health systems deploy Commure's Ambient AI and Dictation tools so physicians spend less time on manual note-taking after patient visits.

use_cases.title: Automating revenue cycle operations
use_cases.description: Billing and finance teams use Commure RCM to automate claims processing and reduce denial rates across a health system.
use_cases.audience: Hospital revenue cycle and finance teams

use_cases.title: Improving frontline staff safety
use_cases.description: Facilities use Strongline's wearable badges to give staff a fast way to call for help during safety incidents.

enrichment.short_description: Compare JSON refers to the category of free, browser-based utilities used to diff two JSON documents and highlight added, removed, or changed keys and values. These tools (such as jsondiff.com, jsoncompare.org, and json-diff.com) typically run entirely in the browser, require no signup, and are aimed at developers who need a quick way to spot differences between JSON payloads, config files, or API responses.
enrichment.seo_title: Compare JSON: Free Online JSON Diff and Comparison Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare two JSON documents online for free. Spot added, removed, and changed keys instantly with a browser-based JSON diff and comparison utility.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Compare JSON describes a widely used category of free, web-based utilities that let developers paste, upload, or link two JSON documents and instantly see the differences between them. Rather than being a single branded company with a public corporate history, tools in this category (examples include jsondiff.com, jsoncompare.org, jsoncompare.io, json-diff.com, and jsondiffs.com) are typically small, independently operated developer utilities, several of which are open source, that share a common purpose and feature set. The core function is a semantic JSON diff: instead of a plain text line-by-line comparison, these tools parse both JSON documents into their underlying key-value structure and identify which keys were added, which were removed, and which values changed, even if the raw formatting, whitespace, or key ordering differs between the two documents. This semantic approach matters because two JSON files that are functionally identical can still look completely different as plain text if keys are reordered or whitespace differs, so a naive text diff would produce a lot of false positives. Most Compare JSON style tools also bundle adjacent utilities: JSON validation to check that a document is syntactically correct, JSON formatting and pretty-printing to make minified or compact JSON readable, and options to ignore key order or whitespace when performing the comparison. Common presentation modes include a side-by-side split view, a unified tree view that lets users expand and collapse nested objects and arrays, and color-coded highlighting (commonly green for additions, red for deletions, and yellow or orange for modified values). Input methods typically include pasting raw JSON text directly, uploading two separate JSON files for comparison, or in some tools fetching JSON directly from two URLs, which is useful for comparing API responses across environments such as staging versus production. Nearly all tools in this category are free to use with no account or signup required, and many explicitly advertise that JSON data is processed client-side in the browser rather than being uploaded to a server, which matters for developers comparing sensitive configuration or payload data. Typical use cases include comparing API response payloads before and after a backend change, diffing configuration files across environments, checking internationalization or localization files for missing translation keys, validating that a data migration preserved the correct structure, and reviewing changes to structured data during code review. Because this is a category of similar independent tools rather than a single vendor, specific company details such as founding year, headquarters, team size, and formal pricing tiers are not publicly disclosed in the way they would be for a venture-backed software company; the tools instead function as free public utilities maintained by individual developers or small teams. When evaluating a Compare JSON tool, developers typically look for whether it processes data locally in the browser (for privacy with sensitive payloads), whether it supports large JSON files without freezing, whether it has a semantic diff mode versus a plain text mode, and whether it supports both file upload and direct paste input.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is a Compare JSON Tool
paragraphs: A Compare JSON tool is a free online utility that takes two JSON documents and shows the differences between them in a readable, visual format. Instead of comparing the raw text character by character, most of these tools parse the JSON structure first, so a document with reordered keys or different whitespace is still recognized as equivalent where the underlying data matches.

This category includes multiple similar independently run sites rather than a single company, and the tools are generally aimed at software developers, QA engineers, and API integrators who need a fast way to check whether two payloads, configuration files, or responses differ.

long_form_blocks.heading: Common Ways Developers Use JSON Comparison Tools
paragraphs: A typical workflow is pasting or uploading a before and after version of a JSON file, such as an API response captured before and after a backend deployment, and reviewing a color-coded diff that highlights exactly which fields changed. This is faster and less error-prone than manually scanning long JSON blobs for differences.

Other common uses include comparing internationalization files across languages to catch missing translation keys, verifying that a data export or migration preserved the expected structure, and checking configuration files across development, staging, and production environments for unintended drift.

features.title: Semantic JSON diff
features.description: Compares the underlying key-value structure of two JSON documents rather than raw text, so key reordering or whitespace differences do not create false positives.

features.title: Side-by-side and tree views
features.description: Presents differences in a split-pane or expandable tree layout so nested objects and arrays are easy to navigate.

features.title: Color-coded change highlighting
features.description: Marks added, removed, and modified keys or values with distinct colors for quick visual scanning.

features.title: JSON validation and formatting
features.description: Checks that pasted JSON is syntactically valid and can pretty-print minified JSON before comparison.

features.title: File upload and direct paste input
features.description: Supports comparing two JSON documents by pasting text directly or uploading JSON files.

features.title: Ignore key order option
features.description: Many tools let users ignore key ordering so logically identical documents with different key sequences are treated as equal.

features.title: Client-side processing
features.description: Several tools process comparisons entirely in the browser rather than uploading data to a server, which matters for sensitive payloads.

features.title: No signup required
features.description: Most Compare JSON utilities are usable immediately without creating an account.

pros: Free to use with no account required on most implementations
Semantic comparison avoids false positives from whitespace or key order differences
Fast way to spot changes in large or deeply nested JSON structures
Useful across many workflows including API testing, localization, and configuration review
Many tools process data client-side, which is better for privacy with sensitive payloads
Low barrier to entry, works directly in any modern web browser with no installation
cons: Not a single branded company, so support, roadmap, and reliability vary by which specific site is used
Very large JSON files can slow down or freeze browser-based comparison tools
Feature sets are inconsistent across different sites in this category, so users may need to try more than one tool
Some sites in this category carry ads or upsells for related paid developer tool suites
No official uptime guarantee or support channel since these are typically free utilities, not commercial products
Company information such as ownership and data handling policies is often not clearly documented

faqs.question: What does a Compare JSON tool do
faqs.answer: It takes two JSON documents and shows which keys or values were added, removed, or changed, usually with color-coded highlighting and a side-by-side or tree view.

faqs.question: Is Compare JSON free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, tools in this category are typically free with no signup required.

faqs.question: Does comparing JSON online upload my data to a server
faqs.answer: It depends on the specific tool. Many advertise that comparisons run entirely client-side in the browser, but users handling sensitive data should verify each tool's data handling claims before pasting confidential JSON.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a JSON diff and a plain text diff
faqs.answer: A JSON diff parses the document structure so it can ignore whitespace and key order and compare actual key-value pairs, while a plain text diff only compares raw characters line by line.

faqs.question: Can I compare two JSON files instead of pasting text
faqs.answer: Most tools in this category support uploading two separate JSON files in addition to pasting raw text.

faqs.question: Why would key order matter when comparing JSON
faqs.answer: In plain text, reordered keys look like a large change even if the data is identical; semantic JSON comparison tools can ignore order so only actual data differences are flagged.

faqs.question: Are these tools good for comparing large JSON files
faqs.answer: Performance varies by tool and browser; extremely large files can be slow in some browser-based comparison tools.

faqs.question: Who makes Compare JSON tools
faqs.answer: This is a category of independently operated free developer utilities rather than a single company, so ownership and maintenance vary by specific site.

use_cases.title: API response validation
use_cases.description: Developers compare API responses before and after a backend change to confirm the payload structure did not unexpectedly change.

use_cases.title: Localization file auditing
use_cases.description: Teams compare translation JSON files across languages to catch missing or mismatched keys.

use_cases.title: Configuration drift detection
use_cases.description: Engineers compare configuration JSON across development, staging, and production environments to catch unintended differences.

enrichment.short_description: Compass, Inc. (NYSE: COMP) is a publicly traded, technology-enabled residential real estate brokerage headquartered in New York City. Founded in 2012, it combines a proprietary software platform, including CRM, marketing, and AI tools, with a nationwide network of real estate agents, and completed an all-stock combination with Anywhere Real Estate in January 2026.
enrichment.pricing_model: Commission-based real estate brokerage; technology platform is bundled into the agent commission split rather than sold as separate software
enrichment.starting_price: No separate software fee; agents operate on negotiated commission splits, generally without an additional technology fee
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 3,200 corporate employees, plus tens of thousands of affiliated real estate agents
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Compass Real Estate: Platform, Pricing, and Agent Model Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compass (NYSE: COMP) is a tech-enabled real estate brokerage with an in-house CRM and marketing platform. Learn how its agent model and pricing work.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Compass, Inc. is a publicly traded, technology-enabled residential real estate brokerage that trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker COMP. It was founded in October 2012 in New York City as Urban Compass by Ori Allon, Robert Reffkin, and Avi Dorfman, and is led by co-founder and CEO Robert Reffkin. Note that the name Compass is used by many unrelated products, including JetBrains Compass and various browser and navigation tools; this entry refers specifically to Compass Inc., the real estate brokerage. Compass operates as an owned-brokerage real estate company built around a proprietary technology platform rather than a traditional franchise model. Its software, marketed to agents as the Compass Platform and more recently unified under Compass One (rolled out starting in 2024), bundles a customer relationship management system, a visual client collaboration tool called Collections, a Marketing Center for creating branded digital and print campaigns, a Business Tracker for pipeline and performance analytics, and AI features that draft listing descriptions, assemble comparative market analyses (CMAs), and generate buyer and seller follow-up communications. Compass also offers integrated title, escrow, and mortgage services through affiliated businesses. Unlike a typical software company, Compass does not charge agents a separate line-item fee for its technology; the platform is included as part of the agent's negotiated commission split, with no additional technology surcharge layered on top. Commission splits at Compass are individually negotiated based on the agent's production level, market, and team structure, and some top-producing agents have negotiated splits as favorable as 90/10 in their favor. As of 2026, Compass has grown substantially: in January 2026 it completed an all-stock combination with Anywhere Real Estate Inc. (parent of brands including Coldwell Banker, Century 21, and Sotheby's International Realty), creating a global real estate services company with a presence in every major US city and roughly 120 countries and territories. Following this combination, Compass has approximately 33,000 agents operating directly under the Compass brand across more than 400 offices in 96 markets, and approximately 340,000 agents and associates, generally independent contractors, across its combined and franchised network. As of mid-2026, Compass reported trailing twelve-month revenue of approximately 7.0 billion dollars and a market capitalization of roughly 8.9 billion dollars, with its stock trading around 11 dollars per share in July 2026. Compass positions itself as the largest residential real estate brokerage in the United States by sales volume. Its target customers are real estate agents seeking an integrated technology stack bundled into their brokerage relationship, and home buyers and sellers who work with Compass-affiliated agents and benefit from tools like Compass Collections for browsing curated listings. Competitors in the tech-enabled brokerage space include eXp Realty, Real Brokerage, and Side, while traditional competitors include Coldwell Banker, Keller Williams, and RE/MAX, several of which are now part of the combined Compass-Anywhere entity following the 2026 merger.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Compass
paragraphs: Compass, Inc. is a publicly traded residential real estate brokerage that differentiates itself through a proprietary, agent-facing technology platform rather than operating as a pure franchise brand. Trading on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker COMP, Compass was founded in 2012 in New York City and has grown into one of the largest residential brokerages in the United States by sales volume.

It is important to distinguish this Compass from unrelated software products that share the same name, such as JetBrains Compass or various browser navigation utilities. This profile refers specifically to Compass Inc., the real estate technology and brokerage company.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Compass Technology Platform
paragraphs: Compass built its business around giving agents access to an integrated software suite covering CRM, marketing, and transaction management, rather than requiring them to piece together third-party tools. The platform, unified under Compass One starting in 2024, includes a client relationship management system, a visual collaboration tool called Collections for sharing curated listings with buyers, a Marketing Center for creating branded campaigns, and a Business Tracker for monitoring pipeline and performance.

AI features within Compass One assist agents with drafting listing descriptions, building comparative market analyses, and generating client follow-up communications, aiming to reduce the administrative workload that traditionally falls on individual agents.

long_form_blocks.heading: The 2026 Anywhere Real Estate Combination
paragraphs: In January 2026, Compass completed an all-stock combination with Anywhere Real Estate Inc., the parent company of major franchise brands including Coldwell Banker, Century 21, and Sotheby's International Realty. The deal significantly expanded Compass's footprint, giving the combined company a presence in every major US city and roughly 120 countries and territories through franchise operations.

This merger materially changed Compass's scale, moving it from a large independent brokerage to a global real estate services company spanning both its owned-agent model and Anywhere's franchise network.

features.title: Compass One workspace
features.description: A unified agent and client platform combining CRM, transaction management, marketing tools, and AI assistance in one product.

features.title: Compass CRM
features.description: A client relationship management system built specifically for real estate agent workflows and integrated with marketing automation.

features.title: Collections
features.description: A visual, Pinterest-style workspace where agents and clients collaborate on curated property listings.

features.title: Marketing Center
features.description: Tools for producing branded digital, print, and social media marketing campaigns for listings.

features.title: AI-assisted listing and client communication
features.description: AI tools that help draft listing descriptions, build comparative market analyses, and generate buyer or seller follow-up messages.

features.title: Business Tracker and Insights
features.description: Analytics tools for agents to monitor their pipeline, transactions, and market performance.

features.title: Integrated title, escrow, and mortgage services
features.description: Affiliated services that let Compass support more of the transaction beyond the brokerage relationship.

features.title: Negotiated commission structure
features.description: Commission splits are individually negotiated per agent, with the technology platform included at no separate fee.

pros: Large, well-capitalized, publicly traded company with substantial scale after the 2026 Anywhere Real Estate combination
Proprietary technology platform is bundled into the commission split rather than billed as a separate software fee
Broad brand and franchise reach spanning roughly 120 countries and territories post-merger
AI tools built specifically for real estate workflows like CMAs and listing descriptions
Access to integrated title, escrow, and mortgage services alongside brokerage services
Track record as one of the largest residential brokerages in the US by sales volume
cons: Commission splits are individually negotiated rather than published, making costs less transparent upfront for agents
As a public company, Compass has a history of profitability pressure and stock price volatility since its 2021 IPO
Recent large-scale merger with Anywhere Real Estate introduces integration risk and organizational change
Agents are independent contractors, so income and benefits vary widely and are not guaranteed
Brand name overlaps with many unrelated technology products, which can create confusion in search results
Technology platform quality and adoption can vary by local market office

faqs.question: Is Compass a software company or a real estate brokerage
faqs.answer: Compass is a real estate brokerage that differentiates itself with a proprietary technology platform; it earns revenue primarily through real estate transaction commissions, not software subscriptions.

faqs.question: What does COMP mean on the stock market
faqs.answer: COMP is the New York Stock Exchange ticker symbol for Compass, Inc., the real estate brokerage.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to use Compass as an agent
faqs.answer: Compass does not charge a separate technology fee; costs are built into the agent's negotiated commission split with the brokerage.

faqs.question: When was Compass founded
faqs.answer: Compass was founded in October 2012 in New York City as Urban Compass by Ori Allon, Robert Reffkin, and Avi Dorfman.

faqs.question: Did Compass merge with another company in 2026
faqs.answer: Yes, Compass completed an all-stock combination with Anywhere Real Estate Inc. in January 2026, adding brands like Coldwell Banker, Century 21, and Sotheby's International Realty to its network.

faqs.question: Is this the same Compass as other software tools with that name
faqs.answer: No. There are several unrelated products named Compass, such as JetBrains Compass; this profile refers specifically to Compass Inc., the NYSE-listed real estate brokerage.

faqs.question: How many agents does Compass have
faqs.answer: As of 2026, Compass has approximately 33,000 agents directly under its brand and roughly 340,000 agents and associates across its combined and franchised network following the Anywhere Real Estate merger.

faqs.question: Who is the CEO of Compass
faqs.answer: Robert Reffkin, one of the company's co-founders, serves as CEO of Compass, Inc.

use_cases.title: Independent agent joining a tech-enabled brokerage
use_cases.description: Real estate agents move to Compass to get an integrated CRM, marketing, and AI toolset bundled into their brokerage relationship instead of buying separate software.

use_cases.description: Clients use Compass tools like Collections to browse curated listings and collaborate visually with their agent throughout a transaction.
