use_cases.description: Real estate teams use Compass's marketing, analytics, and transaction tools to manage a larger volume of listings and clients consistently across multiple markets.
use_cases.audience: Real estate teams and brokerage leaders

enrichment.short_description: Compology developed AI-powered, camera-based smart waste and recycling monitoring technology that tracks dumpster fill levels and contamination for haulers, property managers, and Fortune 500 companies. Founded in San Francisco around 2012 to 2013, Compology was acquired by RoadRunner Recycling in October 2022, not by Rubicon Technologies, and its technology now operates as part of RoadRunner's waste data platform.
enrichment.pricing_model: Enterprise B2B, quote-based hardware plus software subscription (historically; now offered through RoadRunner Recycling)
enrichment.starting_price: Not publicly disclosed; historically sold as a custom quote combining camera hardware installation and a recurring monitoring subscription
enrichment.company_size: Formerly an independent startup; now integrated into RoadRunner Recycling, which had roughly 600 employees around the time of the acquisition
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (now part of RoadRunner Recycling, headquartered in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania)
enrichment.seo_title: Compology: Smart Dumpster Monitoring, Acquired by RoadRunner
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compology built AI camera sensors for waste and recycling monitoring. Learn what it does, its acquisition history, and current status under RoadRunner.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Compology was a San Francisco-based startup that built AI-powered, camera-based sensors installed inside commercial dumpsters and recycling containers to monitor fill levels, detect contamination, and verify waste hauling service. The company was founded by Jason Gates and Benjamin Chehebar, with sources placing the founding around 2012 to 2013 and its first commercial camera-based monitoring deployment occurring in 2014 at the University of California, Santa Cruz. Compology operated as a certified B Corporation and described itself as the world's largest waste and recycling smart metering technology company, serving customers including Fortune 500 companies, property management firms, waste hauling companies, waste brokers, cities, and universities. Its core technology combined in-dumpster cameras with computer vision and AI to generate a Contamination Score that flagged when recyclable containers had non-recyclable material mixed in, along with fill-level data that let haulers optimize pickup schedules and property managers verify that a hauler had actually emptied a container as billed, a use case sometimes referred to as service verification. The technology attracted mainstream press attention, including coverage of McDonald's restaurants installing Compology cameras in dumpsters to track and reduce waste and improve sustainability reporting. It is important to correct a common point of confusion: Compology was not acquired by Rubicon Technologies. Instead, Compology was acquired by RoadRunner Recycling, a Pittsburgh-based commercial waste and recycling collection company, in October 2022. RoadRunner, founded in 2014, works with third-party haulers across more than 40 major US cities and all 50 states, and it acquired Compology specifically to bring AI-driven smart metering technology in-house and improve waste data collection across its collection network; the acquisition reportedly more than doubled RoadRunner's employee headcount to around 600 employees at the time. As of 2026, Compology no longer operates as an independent company; its technology and team have been absorbed into RoadRunner Recycling's broader waste and recycling data platform. Separately, Rubicon Technologies, a different waste and recycling technology company, has been divesting parts of its own software and city-services businesses as of early 2026, but this divestiture is unrelated to Compology and should not be confused with the 2022 RoadRunner acquisition. Anyone researching Compology's current ownership or evaluating its technology today should look to RoadRunner Recycling as the operating entity, since Compology as a standalone brand and company has effectively been folded into RoadRunner's operations since the 2022 deal closed.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Compology Did
paragraphs: Compology built AI-powered camera sensors that were installed inside commercial dumpsters and recycling containers. These cameras captured images of the container's contents and used computer vision to estimate fill level and flag contamination, such as recyclable bins containing non-recyclable waste.

The company, founded in San Francisco around 2012 to 2013 by Jason Gates and Benjamin Chehebar, positioned itself as the world's largest waste and recycling smart metering technology company and operated as a certified B Corporation, serving Fortune 500 companies, property managers, waste haulers, cities, and universities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Acquired Compology: RoadRunner Recycling, Not Rubicon
paragraphs: Compology was acquired by RoadRunner Recycling in October 2022, not by Rubicon Technologies. RoadRunner is a Pittsburgh-based commercial waste and recycling collection company, founded in 2014, that coordinates pickups through a network of third-party haulers across more than 40 major cities in all 50 US states.

RoadRunner acquired Compology specifically to bring AI-based smart metering and container monitoring technology in-house, strengthening its ability to collect data on waste volumes and contamination across its collection network. The deal reportedly more than doubled RoadRunner's employee headcount at the time, to roughly 600 employees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Compology's Current Status
paragraphs: As of 2026, Compology no longer operates as an independent, standalone company. Its camera-based monitoring technology, engineering team, and customer relationships were integrated into RoadRunner Recycling following the 2022 acquisition. Separately, Rubicon Technologies, a different waste tech company, has been divesting parts of its own software and smart-city business as of early 2026, which is unrelated to Compology's ownership history.

features.title: In-dumpster camera monitoring
features.description: Cameras installed inside commercial dumpsters and recycling containers captured images to assess fill level and container status.

features.title: AI-based Contamination Score
features.description: Computer vision analyzed container contents to flag contamination, such as non-recyclable material in a recycling bin.

features.title: Fill-level tracking
features.description: Data on how full a container was helped haulers and property managers optimize pickup frequency and avoid overflow.

features.title: Service verification
features.description: Fill-level and image data let customers verify whether a hauler had actually emptied a container as billed.

features.title: Sustainability and ESG reporting support
features.description: Aggregated waste and recycling data supported corporate sustainability reporting for large enterprise customers.

features.title: B Corp certified operations
features.description: Compology operated as a certified B Corporation, reflecting a stated commitment to social and environmental performance standards.

features.title: Enterprise and multi-site deployment
features.description: The technology was deployed across large, multi-location customers including Fortune 500 retailers and restaurant chains.

pros: Pioneered AI camera-based monitoring for an industry that historically had little real-time visibility into container status
Contamination scoring helped customers reduce recycling contamination penalties and improve sustainability metrics
Adopted by well-known large enterprise customers, including major restaurant chains, indicating real-world enterprise validation
B Corp certification signaled a formal commitment to social and environmental accountability
Technology and team were preserved through acquisition rather than the company shutting down
cons: No longer exists as an independent company, so it cannot be evaluated or purchased as a standalone product today
Current access to the technology requires going through RoadRunner Recycling rather than Compology directly
Historic pricing was not publicly disclosed, making cost comparisons to current alternatives difficult
Camera-based hardware requires physical installation, unlike purely software-based waste management tools
Public information about Compology post-acquisition is limited since it is no longer separately marketed

faqs.question: What did Compology do
faqs.answer: Compology built AI-powered cameras installed inside dumpsters and recycling containers to monitor fill levels and detect contamination for waste haulers, property managers, and large enterprises.

faqs.question: Who acquired Compology
faqs.answer: Compology was acquired by RoadRunner Recycling in October 2022. It was not acquired by Rubicon Technologies, a common point of confusion.

faqs.question: Is Compology still an independent company
faqs.answer: No. As of 2026, Compology operates as part of RoadRunner Recycling rather than as a standalone company.

faqs.question: When was Compology founded
faqs.answer: Sources place Compology's founding around 2012 to 2013, with its first commercial camera-based monitoring deployment occurring in 2014.

faqs.question: What is a Contamination Score
faqs.answer: It is a metric Compology's AI generated by analyzing images from in-container cameras to flag when recyclable containers had non-recyclable material mixed in.

faqs.question: Where was Compology headquartered
faqs.answer: Compology was headquartered in San Francisco, California, prior to its acquisition by Pittsburgh-based RoadRunner Recycling.

faqs.question: Can I still buy Compology's product today
faqs.answer: The underlying technology is now offered through RoadRunner Recycling's waste and recycling data platform rather than sold separately under the Compology brand.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Compology and Rubicon Technologies
faqs.answer: Compology and Rubicon Technologies are separate waste technology companies. Compology was acquired by RoadRunner Recycling, while Rubicon has been a distinct, at times publicly traded, waste technology company that has been divesting parts of its own software business as of 2026.

use_cases.title: Optimizing hauler pickup schedules
use_cases.description: Waste haulers used fill-level data from Compology's cameras to schedule pickups based on actual container fullness rather than fixed routes.

use_cases.title: Verifying billed waste services
use_cases.description: Property managers and large retail chains used image and fill data to confirm haulers had actually emptied containers as invoiced.
use_cases.audience: Property managers and multi-location retail or restaurant operators

use_cases.title: Reducing recycling contamination
use_cases.description: Enterprises used the Contamination Score to identify and correct recurring recycling contamination issues at specific sites.

enrichment.short_description: Components.studio was an online IDE for building, previewing, and testing web components, built by the French design tooling company divRIOTS. It was discontinued on June 1, 2025 along with sibling products Webcomponents.dev and Backlight.dev, as divRIOTS shifted its entire focus to a suite of Figma plugins; the domain now redirects to divriots.com.
enrichment.pricing_model: Discontinued as a standalone product (was previously part of a free and paid Backlight/divRIOTS subscription suite)
enrichment.starting_price: Not applicable; product discontinued as of June 1, 2025
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 17 employees at divRIOTS as of mid-2024
enrichment.seo_title: Components.studio: What It Was and Why It Was Shut Down
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Components.studio was a web component IDE by divRIOTS, discontinued in June 2025. Learn what it offered and where its technology went.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Components.studio was an online integrated development environment (IDE) built specifically for creating, previewing, and testing web components, marketed with the tagline the Component IDE for Web Platform Developers. It was created by divRIOTS, a French software company founded in 2019 by CEO Georges Gomes along with co-founders Francois Valdy and Gregory Quinio, headquartered near Paris in Colombes, France. divRIOTS raised a 3 million dollar financing round led by XAnge, joined by angel investors including Laurent Useldinger, Thibaud Elziere, Eduardo Ronzano, Nicolas Steegmann, Frederic Montagnon, and Julien Romanetto, and as of mid-2024 the company had around 17 employees. Components.studio was one of three closely related developer tools that divRIOTS operated together: Components.studio and its sibling Webcomponents.dev served as browser-based coding environments for building and testing individual web components across many different frameworks and libraries, including plain HTMLElement, Atomico, Lit, TypeScript-based approaches, Omi, FAST, Haunted, Salesforce Lightning Web Components (LWC), Preact, Stencil, Svelte, and Vue 3, letting developers compare coding style, bundle size, and runtime performance of the same component built multiple ways. The third product, Backlight.dev, was positioned as a more complete, code-side design system SaaS platform for front-end teams, letting design and engineering collaborate on a shared component library with features like visual pull request rendering. As of June 1, 2025, divRIOTS officially shut down all three of these products, Components.studio, Webcomponents.dev, and Backlight.dev, citing the growing success of a separate line of business: a suite of more than 20 Figma plugins under the divRIOTS brand that automate design-to-code workflows, such as importing HTML, images, and various file formats (including PDF, PSD, Adobe Illustrator, and CAD or 3D models) directly into Figma, along with AI-assisted image editing tools like background removal and upscaling, plus APIs for programmatic file conversion. The company's stated rationale was to focus all engineering effort on the Figma plugin ecosystem rather than continuing to maintain three separate online IDE products. As of 2026, visiting components.studio redirects to divriots.com, where the company markets its current suite of Figma plugins and browser extension under the positioning statement We Supercharge Figma, offering a single unified subscription called divRIOTS ONE that bundles access to its Figma tooling. One notable exception to the shutdown was LWC.studio, a dedicated online IDE specifically for editing Salesforce Lightning Web Components using the Lightning Design System, which divRIOTS introduced as a narrower successor covering at least part of the use case that the broader Components.studio and Webcomponents.dev platforms previously served. Anyone researching Components.studio today should treat it as a discontinued product: it can no longer be used for live component development or preview, and prospective users looking for a similar web component development environment should look at current alternatives or consider divRIOTS' narrower LWC.studio offering if their use case is specifically Salesforce Lightning Web Components.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Components.studio Was
paragraphs: Components.studio was a browser-based IDE that let web developers write, preview, and test individual web components without setting up a local build environment. It supported a wide range of underlying frameworks and libraries, from vanilla HTMLElement to Lit, Stencil, Svelte, Vue 3, and Salesforce Lightning Web Components, and allowed developers to compare the same component built in different frameworks side by side, including differences in bundle size and performance.

It was developed by divRIOTS, a Paris-area software company founded in 2019, as one of three related developer tools alongside Webcomponents.dev, a very similar sibling product, and Backlight.dev, a more complete code-side design system platform for front-end teams to collaborate on shared component libraries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Why Components.studio Was Discontinued
paragraphs: divRIOTS shut down Components.studio, along with Webcomponents.dev and Backlight.dev, on June 1, 2025. The company stated that it made this decision because of the growing success of its separate suite of more than 20 Figma plugins, which automate design-to-code and file conversion workflows inside Figma, and chose to concentrate all of its resources on that product line instead of continuing to run three separate online IDE products.

As of 2026, the components.studio domain redirects visitors to divriots.com, where the company now sells access to its Figma plugin suite and browser extension through a unified subscription called divRIOTS ONE.

features.title: Multi-framework web component editor (historical)
features.description: Let developers write and preview web components across frameworks including Lit, Stencil, Svelte, Vue 3, and vanilla HTMLElement in one browser-based editor.

features.title: Side-by-side framework comparison (historical)
features.description: Allowed comparing the same component implemented in different frameworks for differences in code style, bundle size, and performance.

features.title: Live in-browser preview (historical)
features.description: Rendered components live in the browser without requiring a local development environment setup.

features.title: Support for Salesforce Lightning Web Components (historical)
features.description: Included templates and tooling for building LWC-based components, a use case later carried forward by the successor tool LWC.studio.

features.title: Integration with sibling design system tooling (historical)
features.description: Worked alongside Backlight.dev and Webcomponents.dev as part of a connected suite of divRIOTS developer products.

features.title: Now discontinued
features.description: As of June 1, 2025, the product is no longer operational; the domain redirects to divRIOTS' current Figma plugin business.

pros: Previously offered a convenient way to prototype web components across many frameworks without local setup
Supported comparison of the same component built in multiple frameworks, useful for framework evaluation
Backed by an established team at divRIOTS with prior design system tooling experience
Some of its use case, specifically Salesforce Lightning Web Components, is still served by the successor tool LWC.studio
divRIOTS remains an active, funded company continuing to build developer and design tooling, just in a different product area
cons: The product no longer exists and cannot be used today
No migration path is documented for developers who relied on it for general web component prototyping outside of the Salesforce LWC use case
Historical documentation and support content may no longer be accessible now that the product is shut down
Teams that built workflows around Components.studio must now find alternative tools
divRIOTS has fully pivoted its business model to Figma plugins, so no future updates to Components.studio should be expected

faqs.question: Is Components.studio still available
faqs.answer: No. Components.studio was shut down on June 1, 2025, and the domain now redirects to divriots.com, where the company sells its current Figma plugin suite.

faqs.question: Who made Components.studio
faqs.answer: Components.studio was built by divRIOTS, a software company founded in 2019 and headquartered near Paris, France.

faqs.question: Why did divRIOTS shut down Components.studio
faqs.answer: divRIOTS cited the growing success of its Figma plugin business and chose to focus all resources there instead of maintaining Components.studio, Webcomponents.dev, and Backlight.dev.

faqs.question: What was Components.studio used for
faqs.answer: It was an online IDE for building, previewing, and testing web components across many JavaScript frameworks and libraries without a local development setup.

faqs.question: Is there a replacement for Components.studio
faqs.answer: divRIOTS introduced LWC.studio as a narrower successor specifically for Salesforce Lightning Web Components; there is no direct general-purpose replacement from divRIOTS for the broader multi-framework use case.

faqs.question: What does divRIOTS do now
faqs.answer: divRIOTS now focuses on a suite of more than 20 Figma plugins and a browser extension that automate design-to-code and file conversion workflows, sold under a unified subscription called divRIOTS ONE.

faqs.question: Were Webcomponents.dev and Backlight.dev also shut down
faqs.answer: Yes, both were discontinued on the same date, June 1, 2025, as part of divRIOTS' pivot to focus entirely on its Figma plugin business.

faqs.question: Was Components.studio free or paid
faqs.answer: It was part of divRIOTS' broader product suite, which historically included both free tiers and paid subscription plans before being discontinued.

use_cases.title: Prototyping web components without local setup (historical)
use_cases.description: Developers used Components.studio to quickly prototype and preview web components directly in the browser before it was discontinued.

use_cases.title: Comparing framework implementations (historical)
use_cases.description: Teams evaluating which web component framework to adopt used the tool to compare the same component built multiple ways.

use_cases.title: Building Salesforce Lightning Web Components
use_cases.description: Developers needing an online IDE for Salesforce LWC development can now use divRIOTS' successor tool, LWC.studio, which continues this narrower use case.

enrichment.short_description: Composio is a developer platform that gives AI agents and large language models secure, pre-built access to more than a thousand external apps and APIs, handling authentication, tool orchestration, and sandboxed execution. Founded in 2023 and headquartered in San Francisco, it raised a 25 million dollar Series A in 2025 led by Lightspeed Venture Partners.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium, usage-based subscription tiers priced by monthly tool calls, plus custom Enterprise pricing
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier available (20,000 tool calls per month); paid plans start at 29 dollars per month
enrichment.seo_title: Composio Pricing and Features: AI Agent Integration Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Composio connects AI agents to 1,000+ apps with managed auth and tool orchestration. See pricing tiers, features, and how it compares.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Composio is an infrastructure platform, founded in 2023 and headquartered in San Francisco, California, that solves a specific problem for developers building AI agents: connecting large language models to external applications and APIs without manually implementing OAuth flows, rate limiting, and custom API wrappers for every service an agent needs to use. The platform provides access to more than a thousand pre-built integrations, referred to as toolkits, covering common business and developer tools, and it manages the authentication layer (OAuth 2.0, API keys, and JWT tokens), encrypted credential storage, and sandboxed execution environments so agents can call external tools safely without direct access to raw credentials. Composio supports the Model Context Protocol (MCP), a standard for connecting AI assistants to external tools and data sources, and offers an MCP Gateway that lets developers expose Composio's toolkits to MCP-compatible clients such as Cursor, Windsurf, and Claude. In February 2026, Composio open-sourced its Agent Orchestrator, a framework intended to move agent development beyond simple ReAct-style reason-and-act loops toward more structured, stateful multi-agent workflows, reflecting the company's broader ambition to provide not just tool connectivity but also orchestration infrastructure for production AI agents. Composio is headquartered at 970 Hayes Street in San Francisco and, as of mid-2026, has a workforce reported at approximately 70 employees, though some business directories list a smaller range of 11 to 50 employees, reflecting the natural lag in third-party employee counts for a fast-growing startup. The company raised a 25 million dollar Series A round in July 2025, led by Lightspeed Venture Partners, with participation from SV Angel, Blitzscaling Ventures, Operator Partners, Agent Fund (backed by Yohei Nakajima), and existing investors Elevation Capital and Together Fund. Notable angel investors include Guillermo Rauch, CEO of Vercel, Dharmesh Shah, co-founder and CTO of HubSpot, Gokul Rajaram, and Soham Mazumdar, co-founder of Rubrik, bringing Composio's total disclosed funding to approximately 29 million dollars. Composio's pricing follows a usage-based tiered model built around monthly tool calls. The Free tier includes 20,000 tool calls per month with community support at no cost. The next tier, priced at 29 dollars per month, includes 200,000 tool calls per month with email support, and additional usage beyond the included allotment is billed at roughly 0.299 dollars per 1,000 calls. A higher tier priced at 229 dollars per month includes 2 million tool calls per month with Slack-based support, with overage billed at roughly 0.249 dollars per 1,000 calls. An Enterprise tier is available with custom quoted pricing that adds dedicated user accounts, custom SLAs, SOC 2 compliance support, adjustable API volume, and VPC or on-premises deployment options for organizations with stricter security or compliance requirements. Composio's target customers are engineering teams building AI agents, whether internal automation agents, customer-facing AI assistants, or agentic products, who want to avoid building and maintaining custom integration code for every third-party service their agent needs to call. It competes in the emerging category of agent-native integration platforms, distinct from traditional automation and integration-platform-as-a-service (iPaaS) tools like Zapier, which were designed primarily for human-triggered workflow automation rather than autonomous AI agent tool calling.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Composio
paragraphs: Composio is infrastructure for AI agents that need to interact with the outside world. Rather than every team building custom authentication flows, rate limiting, and API wrappers for each external service their AI agent needs to use, Composio provides more than a thousand pre-built toolkits that handle this integration work, along with managed authentication and sandboxed execution so agents can call tools safely.

Founded in 2023 and based in San Francisco, Composio positions itself specifically for the agentic AI era, supporting protocols like the Model Context Protocol (MCP) that let AI assistants such as those built on Claude, Cursor, or Windsurf discover and call external tools in a standardized way.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Composio Pricing Works
paragraphs: Composio prices its platform based on monthly tool call volume rather than seats or a flat subscription. The Free tier includes 20,000 tool calls per month with community support. The next paid tier, at 29 dollars per month, unlocks 200,000 monthly tool calls with email support, and a higher 229 dollar per month tier provides 2 million monthly tool calls with Slack support.

Usage beyond each tier's included allotment is billed per additional 1,000 tool calls, and organizations with stricter compliance, deployment, or support needs can move to a custom-quoted Enterprise plan that adds SOC 2 support, dedicated accounts, and VPC or on-premises deployment options.

long_form_blocks.heading: Funding and Company Growth
paragraphs: Composio raised a 25 million dollar Series A round in July 2025 led by Lightspeed Venture Partners, with participation from firms including SV Angel, Blitzscaling Ventures, and Operator Partners, alongside existing investors Elevation Capital and Together Fund. Notable individual backers include Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch and HubSpot co-founder Dharmesh Shah.

The company continued expanding its product surface into 2026, open-sourcing an Agent Orchestrator framework in February 2026 aimed at helping developers build more structured, stateful multi-agent systems rather than relying solely on simple reason-and-act loops.

features.title: 1,000+ pre-built app integrations
features.description: Provides ready-made toolkits for AI agents to connect to over a thousand external applications and APIs without custom integration code.

features.title: Managed authentication
features.description: Handles OAuth 2.0, API key, and JWT-based authentication flows so developers do not need to build and maintain auth logic for every integration.

features.title: Encrypted credential storage
features.description: Stores connected account credentials securely so agents never handle raw secrets directly.

features.title: Sandboxed tool execution
features.description: Runs agent tool calls in isolated environments for safer testing and production use.

features.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP) support
features.description: Offers an MCP Gateway that exposes Composio's toolkits to MCP-compatible AI clients like Claude, Cursor, and Windsurf.

features.title: Agent Orchestrator framework
features.description: An open-sourced framework, released in February 2026, for building structured, stateful multi-agent workflows beyond basic reason-and-act loops.

features.title: Usage-based tiered pricing
features.description: Pricing scales with monthly tool call volume, from a free tier through paid plans to custom Enterprise contracts.

features.title: Enterprise deployment options
features.description: Enterprise customers can access SOC 2 compliance support, dedicated accounts, and VPC or on-premises deployment.

pros: Very large library of over 1,000 pre-built integrations reduces custom integration engineering work
Native support for the Model Context Protocol keeps it aligned with how modern AI coding and agent tools connect to external services
Managed authentication and sandboxed execution reduce security risk compared to building integrations from scratch
Transparent, published usage-based pricing with a usable free tier for testing
Backed by reputable investors including Lightspeed Venture Partners and well-known individual angels
Actively shipping new infrastructure, such as the open-sourced Agent Orchestrator, indicating ongoing product investment
cons: Usage-based pricing on tool calls can become expensive at high volume compared to a flat subscription
As a startup founded in 2023, the company has a shorter track record than legacy integration platforms
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a custom sales conversation
Reliance on a third-party integration layer introduces a dependency that agent-building teams must factor into their own reliability planning
Employee count estimates vary across sources, making it harder to gauge company scale precisely
A scheduled pricing update noted for August 2026 means current tier pricing may not remain stable long-term

faqs.question: What does Composio do
faqs.answer: Composio gives AI agents and large language models pre-built, authenticated access to more than 1,000 external apps and APIs, handling OAuth, credential storage, and sandboxed tool execution.

faqs.question: How much does Composio cost
faqs.answer: Composio offers a free tier with 20,000 tool calls per month, a 29 dollar per month tier with 200,000 calls, a 229 dollar per month tier with 2 million calls, and custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Does Composio support the Model Context Protocol (MCP)
faqs.answer: Yes, Composio provides an MCP Gateway that exposes its toolkits to MCP-compatible AI clients such as Claude, Cursor, and Windsurf.

faqs.question: When was Composio founded
faqs.answer: Composio was founded in 2023 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Who has invested in Composio
faqs.answer: Composio raised a 25 million dollar Series A in July 2025 led by Lightspeed Venture Partners, with participation from SV Angel, Elevation Capital, Together Fund, and angel investors including Guillermo Rauch and Dharmesh Shah.

faqs.question: What is the Composio Agent Orchestrator
faqs.answer: It is an open-source framework Composio released in February 2026 designed to help developers build structured, stateful multi-agent workflows beyond simple reason-and-act agent loops.

faqs.question: How is Composio different from Zapier
faqs.answer: Composio is built specifically for AI agents calling tools programmatically with managed authentication and sandboxing, while Zapier is primarily an integration-platform-as-a-service designed around human-triggered, no-code workflow automation.

faqs.question: Does Composio offer an Enterprise plan
faqs.answer: Yes, Composio offers a custom-quoted Enterprise plan with dedicated accounts, SOC 2 compliance support, adjustable API volume, and VPC or on-premises deployment.

use_cases.title: Building production AI agents that call external tools
use_cases.description: Developers use Composio so their AI agents can securely call services like email, calendars, CRMs, and other business apps without writing custom integration code for each one.

use_cases.title: Adding MCP-based tool access to coding assistants
use_cases.description: Teams use Composio's MCP Gateway to give AI coding tools like Cursor or Claude structured access to external services during development workflows.
use_cases.audience: Developer tooling and platform engineering teams

use_cases.title: Orchestrating multi-agent workflows
use_cases.description: Organizations building more complex agentic systems use Composio's Agent Orchestrator to coordinate multiple AI agents through structured, stateful workflows rather than simple single-agent loops.

features: Additional usage billed at approximately 0.299 dollars per 1,000 calls
Additional usage billed at approximately 0.249 dollars per 1,000 calls
Custom SLA and SOC 2 compliance support

enrichment.short_description: Conky is a free, open-source, lightweight desktop system monitor for Linux, FreeBSD, and OpenBSD that displays real-time system statistics like CPU, memory, disk, network, and battery status directly on the desktop. Originally forked from Torsmo in 2005 by Brenden Matthews, it is highly configurable and extensible with built-in Lua scripting.
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable; open-source community project maintained by volunteer contributors, not a company
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable; distributed open-source project with no corporate headquarters
enrichment.seo_title: Conky: Free Lightweight System Monitor for Linux Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Conky is a free, open-source desktop system monitor for Linux and BSD. See its features, configuration options, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Conky is a free and open-source desktop system monitor originally built for the X Window System, with support extended over time to Wayland and other display environments, and it runs on Linux, FreeBSD, and OpenBSD. Development began in 2005 when Brenden Matthews forked the project from an earlier tool called Torsmo to create a more customizable and extensible system monitor. Between 2005 and 2010, the project matured through versions 1.0 through 1.7.2, adding core capabilities like enhanced network monitoring and basic scripting support. Conky is licensed under a mix of terms reflecting its history: the original torsmo-derived code remains BSD licensed, while new code contributed to Conky since the fork has been released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPL 3.0). The current active codebase is maintained on GitHub under the repository brndnmtthws/conky, described as a light-weight system monitor for X, Wayland, and other display environments. As an open-source community project rather than a commercial company, Conky has no headquarters, no disclosed employee count, and no pricing plans; it is free to download, use, and modify by anyone under its open-source licenses. Functionally, Conky displays real-time system information directly on the desktop as unobtrusive text or graphical widgets, rather than in a separate application window with a graphical toolkit. It can monitor CPU usage and temperature, memory and swap usage, disk storage, network interface activity, battery power levels, running processes, system uptime, and incoming system messages. Beyond core hardware and OS metrics, Conky supports monitoring for email inboxes, Arch Linux package update availability, and popular music players including MPD, XMMS2, BMPx, and Audacious, as well as weather updates and news headlines when configured with appropriate data sources or scripts. A defining technical characteristic of Conky is that it draws its output directly in an X window rather than relying on a high-level widget toolkit like GTK or Qt, which keeps its resource consumption relatively low compared to more heavyweight desktop widget systems, making it popular among users who want detailed system information without a heavy performance cost. Conky is extensible through built-in Lua scripting support, allowing advanced users to build custom modules, animations, and data sources beyond the metrics Conky tracks natively, as well as through integration with external scripts and programs written in any language that can output text Conky can display. Configuration is handled through a text-based configuration file, giving users fine-grained control over layout, fonts, colors, update intervals, and which system metrics are displayed, though this text-based approach means new users typically face a steeper learning curve than point-and-click widget tools. Conky has been recognized in the Linux community for its quality and utility, with Linux Magazine describing it as one of the best-maintained and most useful programs in the open-source world. It is commonly used by Linux desktop enthusiasts, particularly those who customize their desktop environment or window manager setup, to display an at-a-glance system dashboard directly on their wallpaper or desktop background. Typical alternatives in the same space include Conky's own ancestor Torsmo, as well as other desktop widget and system monitoring tools like GKrellM and various desktop-environment-specific system monitor applets, though Conky remains distinctive for its deep configurability and low resource footprint.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Conky
paragraphs: Conky is a free, open-source system monitor that displays real-time desktop widgets showing detailed statistics about a computer's hardware and software state, including CPU load, memory and swap usage, disk space, network activity, temperatures, battery level, and running processes. It runs on Linux, FreeBSD, and OpenBSD and was originally built for the X Window System, with support later extended to Wayland environments.

The project began in 2005 when developer Brenden Matthews forked it from an earlier system monitor called Torsmo, aiming to create a more flexible and extensible tool. Conky has since become a staple of the Linux desktop customization community, particularly among users of tiling window managers and heavily customized desktop setups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Configuration and Extensibility
paragraphs: Conky is configured through a plain text configuration file rather than a graphical settings panel, giving experienced users precise control over layout, colors, fonts, update frequency, and exactly which metrics are shown. This text-based approach favors flexibility and low resource usage over ease of onboarding for new users.

Built-in Lua scripting support lets advanced users extend Conky well beyond its native metrics, building custom widgets, animations, or integrations with external data sources such as weather services, email inboxes, or music player status for players like MPD and Audacious.

long_form_blocks.heading: Licensing and Project History
paragraphs: Because Conky began as a fork of Torsmo, its licensing reflects that history: code inherited from Torsmo remains under the BSD license, while all new code written for Conky since the fork is released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPL 3.0). The project is maintained as an open-source repository on GitHub under brndnmtthws/conky, with ongoing community contributions rather than a single commercial owner.

features.title: Real-time CPU and memory monitoring
features.description: Displays live CPU usage, temperature, memory, and swap statistics directly on the desktop.

features.title: Disk, network, and battery monitoring
features.description: Tracks disk storage usage, network interface activity, and battery power levels.

features.title: Lightweight, direct X-window rendering
features.description: Draws output directly in an X window instead of using a heavier GUI toolkit, keeping resource usage low.

features.title: Built-in Lua scripting
features.description: Supports Lua scripts for building custom widgets, data sources, and visual effects beyond Conky's native metrics.

features.title: Music player integration
features.description: Displays now-playing information from popular players including MPD, XMMS2, BMPx, and Audacious.

features.title: Email and system message display
features.description: Can show inbox status and system messages alongside hardware statistics.

features.title: Extensive text-based configuration
features.description: Fully configurable via a plain text configuration file covering layout, fonts, colors, and update intervals.

features.title: Cross-platform Unix support
features.description: Runs on Linux, FreeBSD, and OpenBSD, with support for both X and Wayland display environments.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
Very low resource footprint compared to widget systems built on heavier GUI toolkits
Extremely configurable, supporting near-total control over what is displayed and how it looks
Extensible through Lua scripting for advanced or custom use cases
Long track record and active community maintenance since 2005
Well regarded in the Linux community as a stable, useful tool
cons: Text-based configuration file has a steep learning curve for users unfamiliar with config syntax
No official graphical configuration editor is bundled, unlike some competing widget tools
Primarily targets X and Wayland environments, so it is not designed for Windows or macOS
Documentation and theming resources are scattered across community wikis and forums rather than a single polished source
Visual style requires manual configuration to look polished, unlike some modern desktop widget tools with built-in themes
As a volunteer-maintained project, feature development pace can vary over time

faqs.question: What is Conky used for
faqs.answer: Conky is used to display real-time system statistics like CPU usage, memory, disk space, network activity, and battery level as desktop widgets on Linux, FreeBSD, and OpenBSD.

faqs.question: Is Conky free
faqs.answer: Yes, Conky is free and open source, licensed under a combination of the BSD license for original Torsmo-derived code and GPL 3.0 for newer code.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Conky support
faqs.answer: Conky supports Linux, FreeBSD, and OpenBSD, and works with both the X Window System and Wayland.

faqs.question: Who created Conky
faqs.answer: Conky was created by Brenden Matthews in 2005 as a fork of an earlier system monitor called Torsmo.

faqs.question: Does Conky support scripting
faqs.answer: Yes, Conky includes built-in Lua scripting support, letting advanced users build custom widgets and data sources beyond its native monitoring features.

faqs.question: How is Conky configured
faqs.answer: Conky is configured through a plain text configuration file that controls layout, fonts, colors, update intervals, and which metrics are displayed.

faqs.question: Does Conky use a lot of system resources
faqs.answer: No, Conky is designed to be lightweight because it draws directly in an X window rather than relying on a heavier graphical toolkit, which keeps resource consumption relatively low.

faqs.question: Is Conky available for Windows or macOS
faqs.answer: No, Conky is built for Unix-like systems, specifically Linux, FreeBSD, and OpenBSD, and is not natively available for Windows or macOS.

use_cases.title: Customizing a Linux desktop with system stats
use_cases.description: Users of tiling window managers and heavily customized desktop environments run Conky to display an at-a-glance dashboard of CPU, memory, and network usage on their wallpaper.

use_cases.title: Monitoring server hardware locally
use_cases.description: System administrators use Conky on Linux or BSD workstations to keep an always-visible view of resource usage during local system administration tasks.

use_cases.title: Building custom desktop widgets with scripts
use_cases.description: Advanced users write Lua scripts or external programs to feed custom data, such as weather or music player status, into a personalized Conky display.
use_cases.audience: Advanced Linux users and script developers

enrichment.short_description: ConnectyCube is a backend-as-a-service platform that provides ready-made SDKs and APIs for in-app chat, peer-to-peer and group video calling, and push notifications. Founded in 2018 and headquartered in Berlin, Germany, it supports iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Cordova, NativeScript, and Web, and offers tiered pricing from a free plan up to an Enterprise plan with HIPAA compliance.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium, tiered monthly subscription based on active users and storage, plus a custom Enterprise tier
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier available for up to 1,000 users; paid plans start at 39 dollars per month
enrichment.company_size: Small to mid-size team (exact headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: ConnectyCube Pricing and Features: Chat and Video Calling API
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ConnectyCube offers chat, video calling, and push notification SDKs for developers. Compare pricing tiers, features, and platform support.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ConnectyCube is a communications backend-as-a-service (BaaS) platform that gives app developers ready-made SDKs and server-side APIs for adding real-time chat, video and voice calling, and push notifications to their applications without building this infrastructure from scratch. The company was founded on May 9, 2018 and is headquartered in Berlin, Germany. ConnectyCube positions its platform as serving both traditional app developers and, more recently, teams building AI agents that need chat and calling capabilities, reflected in its current tagline describing itself as offering chat and calling SDKs for developers and AI agents. On the technical side, ConnectyCube provides native and cross-platform SDKs covering iOS (Swift and Objective-C), Android (Java and Kotlin), React Native, Flutter, Apache Cordova, NativeScript, and JavaScript for the Web, giving development teams a consistent integration path regardless of their client technology stack. Core capabilities include one-to-one and group text chat with typing indicators and delivery receipts, peer-to-peer and multi-party (conference) audio and video calling, push notification delivery, user authentication and authorization, chat file and media attachments, end-to-end encryption for sensitive communications, and automated chat bot support for building conversational experiences. ConnectyCube's pricing follows a freemium, tiered subscription model priced primarily around the number of monthly active users and storage allowance, rather than a flat per-seat fee. The Free plan supports up to 1,000 users with 5 gigabytes of storage, a 1-month message retention window, peer-to-peer audio and video calls, client-side video call recording, and community support, at no cost. The Hobby plan is priced at 39 dollars per month and raises the ceiling to 10,000 users, 10 gigabytes of storage, and 3 months of retention. The Advanced plan, at 99 dollars per month, supports 25,000 users, 25 gigabytes of storage, 6 months of retention, and adds multi-party conference calling on top of peer-to-peer calls. The Pro plan starts from 199 dollars per month, supports 50,000 users (expandable through additional purchases), 50 gigabytes of storage, custom retention, and adds server-side call recording along with priority community and email support. At the top end, the Enterprise plan starts from 599 dollars per month and includes unlimited storage, custom user limits, data encryption, HIPAA compliance support, a 99.9 percent uptime service level agreement, a dedicated account manager, and dedicated server hosting, making it suitable for healthcare and other regulated industries that need to handle sensitive communications data. Across all tiers, ConnectyCube includes core chat, group messaging, meeting reminder, and administration API access, with higher tiers unlocking more capacity, more advanced recording and compliance features, and higher-touch support. ConnectyCube's target customers are software development teams that need to embed real-time communication features, such as in-app customer support chat, telehealth video visits, marketplace buyer-seller messaging, or social and dating app chat and calling, into their own products without building and maintaining this real-time infrastructure themselves. Its Berlin base and HIPAA-capable Enterprise tier make it a relevant option for companies serving European and US markets with regulated communications requirements, and its multi-platform SDK coverage makes it a practical choice for teams shipping the same communication features across web, iOS, Android, and cross-platform frameworks like React Native and Flutter simultaneously.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is ConnectyCube
paragraphs: ConnectyCube is a backend-as-a-service platform that provides the infrastructure and SDKs developers need to add real-time chat, voice and video calling, and push notifications to their own applications. Instead of building messaging servers, call signaling, media routing, and notification delivery in-house, development teams integrate ConnectyCube's SDKs and call its APIs to get these features working quickly.

Founded on May 9, 2018 and headquartered in Berlin, Germany, ConnectyCube supports a wide range of client platforms including native iOS and Android, React Native, Flutter, Cordova, NativeScript, and Web, giving teams a consistent way to add communication features across different app technology stacks.

long_form_blocks.heading: ConnectyCube Pricing Tiers
paragraphs: ConnectyCube uses a tiered pricing model based primarily on the number of monthly users and storage allowance. The Free tier supports up to 1,000 users and 5 gigabytes of storage with peer-to-peer calling and community support at no cost, making it usable for early-stage testing and small applications.

Paid tiers scale from the 39 dollar per month Hobby plan (10,000 users) through the 99 dollar per month Advanced plan (25,000 users, adds conference calling) and the 199 dollar per month Pro plan (50,000 users, adds server-side recording), up to the 599 dollar per month Enterprise plan, which adds HIPAA compliance, a 99.9 percent SLA, and dedicated hosting for regulated or high-scale use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Supported Platforms and Typical Use Cases
paragraphs: ConnectyCube's SDK coverage spans iOS (Swift and Objective-C), Android (Java and Kotlin), React Native, Flutter, Cordova, NativeScript, and JavaScript for Web, letting teams implement the same chat and calling features consistently regardless of client platform.

Common use cases include adding in-app customer support chat to marketplace or e-commerce apps, powering video visits for telehealth applications (aided by the Enterprise tier's HIPAA compliance support), enabling buyer-seller messaging in marketplace apps, and building chat and calling features for social and community apps.

features.title: One-to-one and group chat
features.description: Real-time text messaging with support for direct conversations and group chat channels.

features.title: Peer-to-peer and conference video calling
features.description: Supports both one-on-one video calls and multi-party conference calls, depending on plan tier.

features.title: Push notifications
features.description: Delivers push notifications across supported mobile and web platforms to keep users engaged.

features.title: Multi-platform SDKs
features.description: Native and cross-platform SDKs for iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Cordova, NativeScript, and Web.

features.title: Call recording
features.description: Client-side recording on lower tiers and server-side recording on Pro and Enterprise plans.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: Supports encrypted communications for applications handling sensitive user data.

features.title: Chat bots and automation
features.description: Provides support for building automated chat bots within the messaging layer.

features.title: HIPAA-compliant Enterprise tier
features.description: The Enterprise plan adds HIPAA compliance support, a 99.9 percent uptime SLA, and dedicated hosting for regulated industries.

pros: Broad SDK coverage across iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Cordova, NativeScript, and Web from a single backend
Usable free tier makes it easy to prototype and test before committing to a paid plan
Enterprise tier offers HIPAA compliance, useful for telehealth and other regulated communication use cases
Combines chat, calling, and push notifications in one platform rather than requiring multiple vendors
Clear, published tiered pricing rather than requiring a sales call for lower tiers
Positions itself for both traditional app developers and newer AI agent use cases
cons: Published pricing tiers cap user counts, meaning fast-growing apps may need to upgrade tiers or negotiate custom terms sooner than expected
Server-side call recording is only available starting at the Pro tier, not on Free, Hobby, or Advanced plans
Enterprise pricing starting at 599 dollars per month may be a significant jump for smaller regulated-industry applications
Exact company headcount and detailed corporate information are not fully publicly disclosed
As a smaller, specialized BaaS provider, it has less brand recognition than larger communication platform vendors
Message retention windows on lower tiers (1 to 3 months) may be too short for some compliance or support use cases

faqs.question: What does ConnectyCube do
faqs.answer: ConnectyCube is a backend-as-a-service platform that provides SDKs and APIs for adding chat, video calling, and push notifications to mobile and web applications.

faqs.question: How much does ConnectyCube cost
faqs.answer: ConnectyCube offers a free tier for up to 1,000 users, with paid plans starting at 39 dollars per month and an Enterprise plan starting from 599 dollars per month.

faqs.question: What platforms does ConnectyCube support
faqs.answer: ConnectyCube provides SDKs for iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Cordova, NativeScript, and Web.

faqs.question: Is ConnectyCube HIPAA compliant
faqs.answer: HIPAA compliance support is available on ConnectyCube's Enterprise plan, which starts from 599 dollars per month.

faqs.question: When was ConnectyCube founded
faqs.answer: ConnectyCube was founded on May 9, 2018, and is headquartered in Berlin, Germany.

faqs.question: Does ConnectyCube support group video calls
faqs.answer: Yes, conference (multi-party) calling is available starting on the Advanced plan and above; the Free and Hobby plans support peer-to-peer calls only.

faqs.question: Can ConnectyCube record calls
faqs.answer: Client-side recording is available on all plans, while server-side recording is available starting on the Pro plan.

faqs.question: Is ConnectyCube suitable for AI agent applications
faqs.answer: Yes, ConnectyCube markets itself as providing chat and calling SDKs for both traditional app developers and teams building AI agents.

use_cases.title: Adding in-app customer support chat
use_cases.description: E-commerce and marketplace apps integrate ConnectyCube's chat SDKs to let customers message support or sellers directly within the app.
use_cases.audience: Product and engineering teams at marketplace and e-commerce apps

use_cases.title: Powering telehealth video visits
use_cases.description: Healthcare app developers use ConnectyCube's Enterprise tier, with HIPAA compliance support, to enable secure video consultations between patients and providers.
use_cases.audience: Telehealth and digital health application developers

use_cases.title: Building social and community app messaging
use_cases.description: Social and dating app developers use ConnectyCube's chat and calling SDKs to power real-time messaging and video features across iOS, Android, and Web.

features: Dedicated account manager and dedicated server hosting
